# Transcrição: 04-Nitro V-Scale - Aggressive Spend Increases with Lower Stability 

- **Fonte:** 04-Nitro V-Scale - Aggressive Spend Increases with Lower Stability 
- **Duração:** 15:43
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:04]

We've talked about V scaling, which is, you know, moderate increases within the day on separate days, etc. But sometimes you need to go faster. Some of you will look at this and say 20% a day, 30% a day, even twice a day. It's just not enough. And actually, some of you will also be in a situation where you're in a sales period and you really need to push the budget faster and you're ready for it. You've got stock, your website's performing well, etc. Take, for example, Black Friday, Valentine's Day, Mother's Day. There could be other periods in the year where you've got a small amount of time to maximize your sales. And there's a few different strategies that I would use in that situation, depending on where the ad account is and how much optimization I've done and how much confidence I have to get great performance. Nitro V scale is vertical scaling, but done at much higher percentage increases, much higher than the 40% that Facebook will tell you. Now, you might look at that and say, if Facebook say you shouldn't go above 40% a day ad increases, spend increases, why would you go against that? Now, everything Facebook tells you is based on what they know of the system, but they cannot know every single scenario for every single business out there. And they have to cover the basis for the majority and the majority of people, unfortunately, on Facebook ads don't really know how to run them effectively. You do because you're following the BPM method. And what I want to show you in this example and structure is how I do fast vertical scaling at the campaign level using CBO and using the power of everything that we've put into the BPM method. Now, if I said to you, do you want to scale from $972 ad spending, your campaign, to $5,000 per day in ad spend? And I can do that for you in nine hours. What do you say? And I'll do it profitably as well. Surely the answer is yes. As long as we're following the growth path and our website can withstand the extra traffic, ourselves, etc. And you're ready to spend $5,000 a day. Some of you will look at that and say, but surely some of your education has said that's too fast. Well, actually, there are cases where you need to go fast as well. For some of you, you know, it could be sales periods, it could be a launch, for example, there's many reasons why, when and where you want to go fast. And I want to give you this example, then show you how we did it as well. So this is the example where on this particular ad account, I was pushing ad spends high based on what I knew about how the auction works, the time of year. So this is Black Friday, Cyber Monday weekend. And I needed to get the spends high in order to get my budgets higher and hit my targets. I had already primed everything for the BPM method, we spent months.

## [03:04 - 06:05]

And, you know, talking about Black Friday in particular, I start many months ahead with planning and testing and optimization. So that by the time I got to this stage, I was confident in everything that we were applying to our scaling. And you can see here that the first budget bump that I did at 10 a.m., which is the bottom row, was from $972 to $2,500. As a percentage increase, that's much larger than you generally do on a single campaign. But I did that and I monitored it and performance continued to run quite well through the day. We changed out a few ads and ran some optimizations by later that evening. And this is ad account time. I then bumped the budget up again because this was closing out the last day of the sale, which was on the Monday. And I really wanted to push it hard. Now, bear in mind, if I'm doing this kind of scaling, I'm watching the account quite carefully. So, you know, in the second row, I've gone from $2.5K to $5K. So I've literally doubled my budget at seven o'clock in the evening. I would never recommend anyone to do that unless you've got certain things already in place because it is super high risk. And you can see here how we took that from day one to day six and the scaling that went in between. And it was purely done through the Nitro V-Scale method. So I wasn't duplicating or introducing necessarily new campaigns or anything like that. I was literally just increasing my budgets up because I wanted to control my scaling in a way that I could build that confidence. So, you know, you could duplicate campaigns, introduce new campaigns, etc. Everything carries a risk. But this was a controlled risk for me because we spent many months gearing up for this to be able to double our ad spend on the account and reach the targets that we're aiming for. And you can see here, actually, between days two and five, we reacted to sales. So on day three and day four, the return on ad spend wasn't as strong. The cost per purchase wasn't as strong. And this is an e-com store selling products at the $100 average order value. And we brought, I brought the budgets back down on day three and day four, ready to push again on day five based on performance. And in this example here, you can see how the budget increases have played out at 11 o'clock, at three o'clock on this particular day to really push that spend up. So the next thing is, if you're looking at this and thinking, I would love to do that, but it carries far too much risk. That's absolutely okay, because this method is not for everyone. I do not recommend that you use this until you have a couple of months of confidence scaling, at least under your belt. And you're able to manage the risk and understand what to do when things go wrong as well. And you've got all the controls in place. You've got your dashboard set up. You've been through the BPM method. You've set up every part of the GTCBO method.

## [06:06 - 09:08]

And you're able to react to things when they go wrong as well. So the general setup for this, we're aiming for faster growth. As I mentioned, we make sure that this GTCBO base is in place. So if you haven't been through that part of the training, have a look at what the parts, the constructs is of GTCBO. We also make sure that the Core 4 grading is 16 or higher. So if you've been through the earlier part of the training and you've graded your own Core 4, which you should always do at the start and then later on to make sure that you're on track. This is just from experience that when a Core 4 is 16 or higher, you are more likely to generate stability and growth with your ad campaigns as well. Also, your audience sizes must be big enough to take the amount of budget increase that you can create. So, you know, for example, we went from $1,000 roughly to $5,000. In your case, that might not be enough. You may have more scope to increase your budgets. We didn't. So that's as far as we could take it. And, you know, I would have loved to have gone from $5,000 to $50,000. We just didn't have the audiences to be able to do that. It really depends on how much scope you've got in your lookalikes and your interests and your broad campaigns, etc. To really push that spend up as well. This is ideal for short promotions. The example I gave you was a Black Friday weekend where we first started scaling up on the Friday. We pushed it higher on the Monday again. And we're really making sure that we can maximize sales for a short period of time because we know there's going to be a drop in sales afterwards as well. And we're also making sure we have stable delivery ahead of the scaling. So this campaign, for example, the ad account had been running for four or five years. We already started priming our campaigns, our promotions, our ads and creatives months in advance. And we were ready with creatives. We were ready with our graduation testing process. We had ads already. If we needed to just switch them through, everything was in place to give us as much success as possible. So, you know, for stable delivery of NitroVScal, you should be generating at least the 50 conversions a week and at least 200 across the lifetime. For us, we had tens of thousands of conversions already. So we had that confidence that we could scale up and we were doing well above the 50 a week in conversions. This plays a big part in NitroVScaling. So we had a real strong base and we were able to scale up in a strong way as well. Our conversion rate optimization and auction optimizations had already been done weeks, if not months in advance. So all the things that we cover in the optimization section, we'd already been applying. So we're not panicking about, you know, site speed and things like that. That's already done. We'd also been through the learning phase of scale testing. We'd also tested out some horizontal scaling ahead of time so that we had our campaigns completely set up as well.

## [09:08 - 12:13]

We were applying multiples in the day of different percentage increases. And you can see that was just one example. 50%, 500% or even more budget bumps, ideally between six and 12 in the early part of the day. And then as a controlled measure, because I'm an expert media buyer, I did it at seven o'clock in the evening. But I'm there monitoring and making sure the performance continues. If you don't have the experience and you're not around at that evening time to monitor your ad account, don't be as aggressive as I was, because you could quickly lose a ton of money as well. And by that time, we had our automated stop loss rules in place as well so that the budget couldn't escape. We use an external tool. You can use Facebook ads rules, for example. And there's pros and cons which are covered in the automation section. But essentially what we wanted to do was really push up those budgets, monitor them closely, run ad sniping, keep a close eye on performance, but have the rules in place to capture any runaway performance and cut any bad spend before it has a big, big negative impact on us. And the funnel lengths play a consideration as well. For us, we were running a typical Ecom funnel. So add into Lander, Lander to add to cart, cart into the checkout, etc. The shorter the conversion path, the more confidence I would have in scaling. So for example, when I've been scaling my own info product ads, I'm able to scale those more effectively and less risk-free with bigger bumps because the paths have been shorter. And so you need to just bear in mind what your funnel looks like ahead of going heavy into this scaling. So if we've just discussed how it's done, the things to put in place, etc. Where an elite media buyer is really looking to earn their keep, I guess, is what to do when things go wrong. So for example, what I want to do is make sure I have my fallout plan in place. So for example, if in a given day, I've gone aggressive with VScale and I've really pushed my budgets up, if performance has dropped compared to what I would expect by 3pm, then I reduce my budgets down as well. I would also consider, depending on your confidence of your product, your marketing, etc. If you are running those higher budgets, so for example, I went from five to 10k overnight. Consider dropping the budget back down to your baseline, which is 5k, for example, because the problem is, if you go from five to 10k and spend really fast and you maintain that 10k and the following day it starts at 10k, you could quite quickly burn through your budget in the early part of the day because Facebook doesn't have the history. So for example, if I've just increased my budget at 9am and let's say 2pm and I've gone from $1000 to $5000, it's picked up the data and the knowledge from 9am onwards, but it doesn't really know what's going to happen midnight onwards.

## [12:13 - 15:18]

So that's a huge risk. So I wouldn't necessarily start my next day at 10k. I might have a rule in place that says at midnight, drop it back down to 5k because that history is already there. Facebook knows that if I've got 5k to spend, this is the pattern of behavior that should occur. Then on the second day, I might do the same budget increase again and then reduce it down. In my case, because of my experience, I would let it run with rules in place and checks and things like that. But for people that haven't run this already, do consider dropping your budgets down because it will enable your ad account to build more confidence and also give you a bit more peace of mind as well. Consider the buyer intent when using this method. I'm generally using this at times of high peak sales. So as I've mentioned, particularly events like Mother's Day, Valentine's, let's say start of the year, January for certain clients, and obviously Black Friday. Again, within launches as well. So if I'm doing a product launch or something like that, then I might consider this as well. But the buyer intent plays a big part because if, for example, it's Black Friday and there's a huge increase in people that want to buy in and buy products on discount or promotion or ahead of Christmas or anything like that, then if I know that's already happening, I have more confidence in scaling faster as well. And also consider v-scaling within the day at smaller amounts as well. So for example, you know, in that example I shared, I went from about 972 to 2.5K to 5K really fast. It doesn't have to be that aggressive as well. So you could go from $1,000 to 2,000 and then from 2,000 to 2.5, et cetera, throughout the day, but continually monitoring. And when you do your bumps, give it at least an hour to look at the data that's coming back, ideally two or three hours to be confident in what you're doing. The other thing about campaign budget optimization is sometimes when you scale too aggressively. So let's say you went from $1,000 to $10,000 a day, CBO won't always spend your budget. Now you might be frustrated by that by saying, I have full confidence. I've followed the whole BPM method, we're in Black Friday or whatever. And I'm really confident I can go from 1K to 10K. There's actually a failsafe that's built into CBO to avoid you losing a ton of money. Because if you think about the flip side, you could go from spending 1K to 10K and 9K is absolutely wasted. And you're going to be hacked off at Facebook and write a letter to them or an email or whatever it is and say, you know, I increased my budget and you wasted it. The fact is there's a user as an advertiser, it's your fault. You decided to spend it and so that it's on you. But some advertisers feel like obviously Facebook is the problem. And so there are some, you know, the failsafe is built in that if you do scale too aggressively, sometimes it won't spend as much as you expect it to spend as well. Just bear that in mind as well. And that's where some of the other strategies come in place, like manual bidding and going for hscale, etc.

## [15:19 - 15:43]

So, you know, I would also test this ahead of your key period and make sure you understand how this works, how to react. You have your controls in place, etc. So that when you're actually using it at the time where you cannot make mistakes, you've already made your mistakes earlier on, you know what to look for and how to make it work. So that when you apply Vscal, it gives you the performance and scaling that you are looking for.
